followUpExplanations.howToImprove: If you need an answer grounded in specific documents, paste the relevant snippets OR use a Project/custom GPT with files attached. Don't guess.
followUpExplanations.example:
Here are the 3 relevant sections from my company's onboarding handbook:
[snippet 1] [snippet 2] [snippet 3]
Using ONLY these sections, answer: what's our policy on remote work for new hires? If the sections don't cover it, say so.

title: AI ethics — three real risks (not the sci-fi ones)
airaIntro: Most 'AI ethics' headlines are sci-fi. The real ethical issues are mundane and you encounter them weekly. Today: the three that matter for your daily use.
learnFirst: Three risks worth knowing: 1) Bias — AI reproduces patterns in training data, including discriminatory ones. 2) Copyright — outputs may resemble copyrighted material; legal status is unsettled. 3) Energy & data — large models consume real water + electricity; your prompts have a footprint.
realWorldScenario: Hana, an entrepreneur, asks AI for 'a CEO photo for a marketing deck'. Every output is a white man in a suit. She didn't notice the first three. Once she did, she understood why she should always specify.

scenes.heading: Bias is the default
scenes.specific: AI reproduces patterns in its training data. If training data over-represents one group, outputs do too. Without specifying, the model picks the statistical mode.
scenes.note: Always specify when you want diversity. The model won't add it on its own.

scenes.heading: Copyright is unsettled
scenes.vague: AI output is free to use.
scenes.specific: Legal status is evolving. Generated text/images may resemble training data; commercial use can carry risk. Check your jurisdiction and your tool's terms.
scenes.note: For commercial work, prefer tools that explicitly grant commercial rights and indemnify users.

scenes.heading: Footprint is real
scenes.specific: A single GPT-4 query uses 10x the energy of a Google search. Aggregated across billions of queries, it adds up.
scenes.note: Use cheaper/smaller models for cheaper tasks. Don't use Claude Opus to summarise a tweet.

airaOutro: Ethics isn't lofty — it's the small things you specify in every prompt. Get the habits right and you'll use AI better than 95% of users.
takeaway: Specify diversity. Right-size models. Mind the footprint.

followUpExplanations.whyScore: Judges check whether prompts that could trigger biased defaults specify diversity, OR whether they right-size the model claim ("use a small model for this").
followUpExplanations.howToImprove: For people/role prompts, add explicit diversity. For simple tasks, mention the smaller model is fine.
followUpExplanations.example: Generate 6 stock-style photos of medical professionals. Mix: 3 different ethnicities, 3 different ages (25-60), even gender split. Setting: modern hospital lobby. Photorealistic.

title: Python — your first line of real code
airaIntro: We start where every Python coder ever started: print. One word, one line, you're a programmer.
learnFirst: `print()` shows text on the screen. Anything inside the parentheses with quotes around it gets shown. That's it for now.
realWorldScenario: Maya is following an online tutorial. The very first line is `print("Hello, world!")`. She runs it. Words appear. She is officially writing Python.

scenes.heading: The simplest possible program
scenes.specific: Write: `print("Hello!")` — exactly. Quotes around the words. Parentheses around the quotes.
scenes.note: Python is picky about brackets and quotes. Match them and it works.

scenes.heading: Variables — give a name to a value
scenes.specific: `name = "Maya"` then `print("Hello, " + name)`. Now the name is reusable.
scenes.note: Variables are nicknames for values. Reusing them keeps your code clean.

scenes.heading: Strings vs. numbers
scenes.specific: `age = 12` (a number). Now `age + 1` works and gives 13.
scenes.note: Quotes mean "treat this as text." No quotes means "treat this as a number."

airaOutro: That's the foundation. `print`, variables, types. Every Python program builds on these three.

title: Python — functions: bottle up logic so you can reuse it
airaIntro: Once you've copy-pasted the same code three times, it's time to make it a function. Today: how, why, and the one trap most beginners hit.
learnFirst: A function is a named block of code you can run again and again. You define it with `def`, name it, give it inputs (parameters), and the body does the work. Inputs are caller-supplied; the body uses them.
realWorldScenario: Lin keeps writing the same 3-line "print a greeting for X" code. Six greetings, eighteen lines. She wraps it in a function. Six greetings, six lines.

scenes.heading: Defining vs. calling
scenes.vague: Define it once but never call it: `def greet(name): print("Hi, " + name)`
scenes.specific: Add `greet("Lin")` BELOW the definition. That's the call. The call is what runs.
scenes.note: `def` builds the function. `greet("Lin")` runs it. Both are needed.

scenes.heading: Return — sending a value back
scenes.vague: `def add(a, b): print(a + b)` — only prints, can't reuse the result.
scenes.specific: `def add(a, b): return a + b`, then `total = add(2, 3)` — now `total` is `5` and you can use it elsewhere.
scenes.note: `print` shows. `return` hands a value back to the caller.

scenes.heading: The classic trap
scenes.vague: `def add(a, b): a + b` — no return, function gives back `None`.
scenes.specific: Always `return` if you want a value. Forgetting `return` is the most common Python beginner bug.
scenes.note: No `return` = no value. The function still ran, but you got nothing back.

airaOutro: Functions are how you stop repeating yourself. Every real Python program is a tower of small functions.

title: Java — the famous Hello World, line by line
airaIntro: Java looks scarier than Python at first. More words. More braces. Today we read the famous Hello World line by line so it stops looking like a wall.
learnFirst: Java needs every program to live inside a class. The entry point is `public static void main(String[] args)`. Inside that, `System.out.println("Hello!");` prints text. The semicolons are required.
realWorldScenario: Sam joins a coding club. The first task is a Hello World in Java. The instructor pastes 5 lines of code. Sam panics. After this lesson, none of those 5 lines look strange anymore.

scenes.heading: The class wrapper
scenes.vague: Just write code at the top, like Python.
scenes.specific: Java needs: `public class Hello { ... your code here ... }`. Everything lives inside a class.
scenes.note: A class is a container. Java refuses to run code that isn't inside one.

scenes.heading: main is the entry point
scenes.vague: Java picks any function to start.
scenes.specific: Java looks for exactly `public static void main(String[] args)`. That's the line that runs first.
scenes.note: Misspell main and the program won't start. The signature is fixed.

scenes.heading: Printing — and the semicolon
scenes.specific: `System.out.println("Hello!");` — semicolon at the end of the statement.
scenes.note: Semicolons end statements in Java. Forget one and the compiler refuses.

airaOutro: Now Hello World in Java is just words you understand. The five lines aren’t scary anymore.

title: Java — classes are nouns, methods are verbs
airaIntro: If you only remember one thing about object-oriented design today: classes are nouns, methods are verbs. The whole language clicks once you see this.
learnFirst: A class is a blueprint for a thing — a Dog, a Car, a User. Its fields are the thing's properties. Its methods are what the thing can do. To use it, you create an instance with `new` and then call methods on it.
realWorldScenario: Jordan models a coffee shop's point-of-sale. Drink, Customer, Order — three nouns. Drink.brew(), Customer.pay(), Order.print() — three verbs. The whole system falls out cleanly.

scenes.heading: Fields = the noun's properties
scenes.vague: Stuff some variables in a class.
scenes.specific: A `Dog` class has `String name; int age;`. Those are facts about a dog.
scenes.note: Pick fields that describe the thing. Skip anything that doesn't.

scenes.heading: Methods = the noun's verbs
scenes.vague: A `Dog` with no actions is just data.
scenes.specific: `void bark()` and `void wagTail()` are things a dog does. Now the class is alive.
scenes.note: Verbs make a class useful. Without methods, it's just a struct.

scenes.heading: Using the class
scenes.vague: Try to call `bark()` directly on the class.
scenes.specific: `Dog rex = new Dog(); rex.bark();` — make an instance first, then call its methods.
scenes.note: `new` builds an instance. The instance owns the methods you can call.

airaOutro: Once you split your problem into nouns and verbs, OOP stops feeling like ceremony. Drink. Brew. Customer. Pay. The system writes itself.
takeaway: Nouns are classes. Verbs are methods.

title: HTML — what tags really are
airaIntro: HTML looks scary because of the angle brackets. It is not. Tags are just labels saying 'this part is a heading' or 'this part is a paragraph'.
learnFirst:
HTML uses tags to label parts of a page. Tags come in pairs: an opening `

` and a closing `

` with content in between. The tag name tells the browser what to render.
realWorldScenario: Maya wants to make her first webpage about her dog. One title, one paragraph, one image. Three tags. That's the whole page.

scenes.heading: Headings
scenes.vague: <bigtext>About my dog</bigtext> — `bigtext` is not a real tag.
scenes.specific:
`

About my dog

` — `h1` is a real heading tag. There's `

` through `

`.
scenes.note: h1 is the biggest. h6 is the smallest. Use real tag names.

scenes.heading: Paragraphs
scenes.specific:
`

He is the best dog ever.

` — wraps a paragraph. Browsers add space around it automatically.
scenes.note:
Wrap each paragraph in `

`. Browsers know what to do with it.

scenes.heading: Self-closing tags
scenes.vague: `` followed by `` — wrong, images don't close.
scenes.specific: `` — image tags self-close. No content between them.
scenes.note: Some tags hold content (need closing). Some don't (self-closing). `img`, `br`, `hr`, `input` are self-closing.

airaOutro: Tags are labels. h1 is a heading label. p is a paragraph label. img is an image label. That’s HTML.

title: HTML — semantic tags + a real form
airaIntro: There's a difference between HTML that works and HTML that's good. Today: semantic tags + how to make a real form people can fill in.
learnFirst: Semantic tags describe what content IS, not just how it looks. Use `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<article>`, `<footer>` instead of `<div>` for everything. Forms use `<form>`, `<label>`, `<input>`, and `<button>`.
realWorldScenario: Deniz is building a sign-up page. Email field, password field, submit button. The form has to actually be ACCESSIBLE — screen readers must understand it.

scenes.heading: Divs vs. semantic tags
scenes.vague:
`
...
` — works visually, but tells the browser nothing.
scenes.specific: `<header><nav>...</nav></header>` — same layout, tells browsers and assistive tech what each part IS.
scenes.note: Same look. Better meaning. Better SEO. Better accessibility. Same effort.

scenes.heading: Form basics
scenes.vague: Just put inputs on the page.
scenes.specific: Wrap them in `<form>`. Each input gets a `<label>`. The submit is a `<button type="submit">`.
scenes.note: A form without `<form>` won't submit. A label without `<label for="...">` confuses screen readers.

scenes.heading: Connecting label to input
scenes.specific: `<label for="email">Email</label> <input id="email" type="email">` — `for` connects to `id`.
scenes.note: Now clicking the label focuses the input. And screen readers announce them together.

airaOutro: Semantic HTML is HTML that means something. Browsers, search engines, and screen readers all reward you for it. Same effort as div soup.
